Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ha, Meem. 'Ain, Seen, Qaf. (1-2)

All 'Ha-Meem' Surahs Share the Same Character and Theme

This is the Qur'anic name of this Surah. It will be recalled that the preceding two Surahs are also named 'Ha-Meem'. Here, 'Ain, Seen, Qaf' is added to it. The sharing of names is proof of the unity of the core theme (amood), and this addition is an indication that in this Surah there are also some specific themes which are not present in the preceding two Surahs; indeed, by looking at the analysis of the contents, these specific themes can be separated.

"Thus does He reveal to you, [O Muhammad], and to those before you - Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (3)

The Teachings of All Prophets Have Been One, and Their Method of Teaching Has Also Been One

'Thus' (Kadhallika) points to those themes which are described in this Surah. A general concept of these themes has been given by the name of this Surah; because of this, pointing to them through 'Kadhalika' is entirely suitable—meaning those matters which are being revealed in this Surah, just as they are being revealed to you, were also revealed to the prophets who came before you. In delivering the meaning, according to the requirement of eloquence, there is an omission (ejaz). The complete statement is as if: 'In this manner does Allah reveal to you, and in this manner did He reveal to those prophets who passed before you.' Several examples of this kind of omission have passed before, and will also come in the future.

'Kadhalika' also points to the unity of the objective and to the identical nature of the method of revelation—meaning Allah Almighty taught all prophets and messengers those very same things whose teaching is being given to you, and He chose the very same method for this teaching which He chose for you; for this reason, from no perspective is there anything in the Qur'an which is a cause of panic for the people. If they are panicking, it is due to the corruption of their own nature.

The unity of the objective is pointed to further below in these words:

'He has ordained for you of religion what He enjoined upon Noah and that which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what We enjoined upon Abraham and Moses and Jesus - to establish the religion and not be divided therein. Difficult upon the polytheists is that to which you invite them...' (13).

From this, it is learned that the same Islam and the same religion of Tawhid to which the Prophet (pbuh) invited was what all prophets (peace be upon them) were commanded to invite to; but the polytheists, in the heat of their tribal bias for their fabricated religion of shirk, stood up as the enemies of this religion of truth, and under the influence of the same bias, the People of the Book also fell into this madness.

The identical nature of the method is pointed to further below in this Surah as follows:

'And it is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil or that He sends a messenger [i.e., angel] to reveal, by His permission, what He wills. Indeed, He is High and Wise. And thus We have revealed to you an inspiration of Our command. You did not know what is the Book or [what is] faith, but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will of Our servants.' (51-52).

This clarified that those who are demanding today: 'If God speaks to the Prophet, why does He not speak to us directly?' or 'Why does He not appear so that they see Him and hear His words?', this is merely their own self-will and bad intellect. Allah Almighty neither speaks to anyone in this manner, nor is it suitable to His majesty to appear in this manner; rather, His way has always been that He chose those of His servants whom He pleased for the special task of prophethood, spoke to them through revelation, and this revelation has a specified rule.

This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh) and to establish final proof against the opponents—showing that since you are inviting to the same religion of truth to which all prophets and messengers invited, you are not presenting anything which is a cause of panic for the people. Those who are panicking are opponents of the invitation of all prophets and are caught in bias and hostility. By the same token, if you cannot make people observe Allah Almighty directly or make others hear Him speaking, this is also no deficiency in your prophethood. You are presenting the revelation of Allah, and whomever Allah speaks to, He speaks through revelation alone. He does not bestow more than this upon anyone.

Referring here to the attributes 'Al-Aziz' (Exalted in Might) and 'Al-Hakeem' (Wise) from the beautiful names of Allah relates to the theme of consolation and also to the theme of warning. Since Allah is 'Al-Aziz', He can suppress the rebellious whenever He wills. If He does not suppress them immediately, He is granting them respite under His wisdom; for this reason, the Prophet (pbuh) must rely on his Exalted in Might, Wise Lord, and refer their matter to Him.

"To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and He is the Most High, the Most Great." (4)

Explanation of the Attribute of being Exalted in Might ('Al-Aziz')

This explains the attribute of being Exalted in Might ('Al-Aziz')—showing that whatever is in the heavens and the earth is His property and in His power; neither is there any partner of anyone in anything, nor is anything outside His sphere of power and choice. He is a highly elevated and highly great Being, and no one has such a status as to be His equal. The meaning is: if He is granting respite to people, it does not mean they have escaped His sphere of choice; and if He has blessed anyone with power and glory, they should not become so arrogant as to dare to see God and speak with Him. The court of Allah is highly elevated, and His Being is highly great.

"The heavens almost break from above them, and the angels exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord and ask forgiveness for those on earth. Unquestionably, indeed, Allah is the Forgiving, the Merciful." (5)

Explanation of 'the Most High, the Most Great' (Al-Aliyy, Al-Atheem)

This describes the height and greatness of the Most High, the Most Great God—showing that under the weight of His greatness, such is the state of the heavens that it appears they will break from above them, and the state of the angels, despite all their proximity, is that out of His fear, they remain continuously active in His praise and glorification and ask forgiveness for the people of the earth. That is: foolish people have associated the angels in divinity and are in the hope that they enjoy such a station before God's court that they will secure great ranks for their worshippers, whereas the state of the angels themselves is that they remain always in His fear, active in praise and glorification.

The difference between 'Tasbeeh' (glorification) and 'Hamd' (praise) has been discussed in its proper place. In 'Tasbeeh', the aspect of declaring purity is dominant, and in 'Hamd', the aspect of establishing praise. That is: they declare Allah Almighty to be pure from all things contrary to His majesty—among which the most prominent is shirk—and characterize Him with all high attributes—among which the most primary is Tawhid.

In 'and ask forgiveness for those on earth' (wa yastaghfiroona liman fil-ard), the same point has been stated which has passed in Surah Ghafir in these words:

'Those [angels] who carry the Throne and those around it exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord and believe in Him and ask forgiveness for those who have believed...' (7).

The Angels' Asking of Forgiveness for the People of the Earth

This shows that the angels ask forgiveness for those among the people of the earth who are believers. Since this was clear, it has been omitted in the verse under discussion. This asking of forgiveness of the angels is their intercession which they are performing before their Lord for the believers. This refutes the polytheists' assumed concept of intercession.

'Unquestionably, indeed, Allah is the Forgiving, the Merciful'—this is a timely warning to the polytheists: 'Open your ears and hear well that the Giver of forgiveness and mercy is Allah alone. If this were in the control of the angels, why would they humbly request Allah Almighty for people's forgiveness?'

"And those who take as protectors other than Him - Allah is Guardian over them; and you are not over them a manager." (6)

A Severe Warning to the Polytheists

This is a highly severe warning to the polytheists and consolation for the Prophet (pbuh)—showing that after these clear proofs, those who have taken protectors besides Allah, and are persistent on their stubbornness despite all warnings, Allah is keeping watch over them to seize them as soon as they complete their respite. After this, the Prophet (pbuh) is consoled: 'God has not placed this responsibility on you that you must force them to walk upon the path of faith. Your responsibility was to deliver the truth, and you have done that, and when it is the command of your Lord, continue doing it. If they do not believe, they themselves will be questioned, not you.' This same theme is expressed below in this Surah as follows:

'But if they turn away - We have not sent you, [O Muhammad], over them as a guardian; upon you is only [the duty of] notification.' (48).

"And thus We have revealed to you an Arabic Qur'an that you may warn the Mother of Cities [i.e., Makkah] and those around it and warn of the Day of Assembly, about which there is no doubt. A party will be in Paradise and a party in the Blaze." (7)

'Thus' (Kadhalika) points to the theme of verse 3 above—meaning: 'In the same manner that We blessed the prophets and messengers before you with Our revelation, We have sent down to you this Qur'an in the Arabic language so that you may warn the central city of Arabia and those around it.'

Describing the Qur'an as 'Arabic' (Arabiyyan) is by way of favor and the final establishment of proof, as has passed in verse 44 of Surah Fussilat—showing that no excuse remains for the Arabs that the religion of Allah was not explained to them in detail in their own language.

The Aspect of the Establishment of Proof in 'Mother of Cities'

'The Mother of Cities' (Umm-al-Qura) refers to Makkah, because 'Umm-al-Qura' means the central city, and in Arabia, only Makkah held this status. Within this word, there is also an aspect of the establishment of proof. If a message is delivered to the people of the central city, it means that the people of that country have been called upon. If, instead of the Mother of Cities, this invitation had arisen from some corner of Arabia, disputers could have said: 'Leaving our chiefs, leaders, and our intelligent class, why did the Qur'an address the common people first; the primary ones to evaluate its truth or falsehood could only be the chiefs of Makkah!'

What is Meant by 'and those around it'?

Some have interpreted 'and those around it' (wama hawlaha) as the entire world. Their view is based on the fact that the Prophet's (pbuh) advent was for the entire world; therefore, not only the surroundings of Makkah or the cities of Arabia are meant, but the entire world. Although it is a reality that the Prophet (pbuh) is the Seal of the Prophets, because of which his advent was for the entire world, this interpretation of 'wama hawlaha' is first an open deviation from the literal limits of the words, and second is not required for the primary objective. The Prophet (pbuh) himself stated that he was sent with two advents: a specific advent, and a general advent. Your specific advent was to the people of Makkah and the Arabs, and you established proof against them directly. As for your general advent, it is to the entire world, and the responsibility of testifying to the religion of truth before the people of the world has been placed by the Qur'an and the Prophet (pbuh) upon the Muslim Ummah until the Resurrection. It is for this responsibility that this Ummah has been blessed by Allah Almighty with the office of 'witnesses of Allah in the earth' (shuhada'a allahi fil-ard). This is the primary duty of this Ummah—that just as the messenger of Allah testified to the religion of truth before this Ummah, they must continuously testify before others. From this duty, this Ummah also received the honor that a group among them will always remain upon the truth, to the extent that they will remain upon the truth even when the poison of falsehood has spread through the veins of the world.

Warning of the General Court

'And warn of the Day of Assembly, about which there is no doubt'—after the general warning, this is the specific warning: warn them specifically of 'the Day of Assembly' (Yawm-al-Jam'), which points to the Day of Judgment. Describing the Day of Judgment as 'the Day of Assembly' warns of the reality: on that Day, Allah Almighty will gather everyone without exception—worshippers and deities, leaders and followers, prophets and their companions, disbelievers and their supporters, and in the presence of all, will make His decision in the full court as to who did what regarding the religion of Allah—who created difference and corruption in it, and who tried to maintain its unity and purity, who deserves reward and who punishment.

'A party will be in Paradise and a party in the Blaze'—this describes the decision of that open court: on that Day, one group, i.e., the believers, will necessarily enter Paradise, and the other, i.e., the disbelievers, will necessarily enter the Blaze.

"And if Allah had willed, He could have made them one nation, but He admits whom He wills into His mercy. And the wrongdoers have not any protector or helper." (8)

Answering a Doubt

Now, this answers a doubt: when Allah Almighty gave people a single religion, why did He not prefer that all remained upon this religion? Why did He give this opportunity for people to create differences in it, resulting ultimately in one group deserving Paradise and the other deserving the Blaze?

The reply given is: if Allah Almighty had willed to do so, He could have done it, and no one could have held His hand. But He did not prefer to force people to accept His guidance; rather, He willed to give people choice and place His guidance before them so that people, utilizing their intellect, choose guidance with their own free will, and become deserving of entering His mercy. In 'He admits whom He wills into His mercy' (yudkhilu man yasha'u fee rahmatihi), the willing (mashee'at) of Allah Almighty is based on His mercy and His justice. For this reason, its meaning is: He will admit into His mercy those whom His justice requires to be admitted. This was clarified with the subsequent words: 'And the wrongdoers have not any protector or helper'—meaning as for those who are wrongdoers and polytheists, they will have neither any protector nor helper; neither will their assumed protectors be of any use to them, nor will they have any army to help them. This shows that only those will remain deprived of Allah's mercy who are wrongdoers and polytheists, and this is because this is the requirement of His justice. His willing is based on His justice, and no one can alter His willing.

This theme is expressed in various places in the Qur'an and has been clarified repeatedly in this book.

For further clarification, we present references of some verses. In Surah Yunus:

'And had your Lord willed, those on earth would have believed - all of them entirely. Then, [O Muhammad], would you compel the people in order that they become believers? And it is not for a soul to believe except by permission of Allah, and He will place defilement upon those who will not use reason.' (Yunus: 99-100).

This shows that the matter of faith and guidance is based on the willing of Allah, and His willing grants the capability of faith to those who utilize their intellect. Those who do not utilize their intellect have their intellect so buried under defilement that they cannot see the light of faith and guidance. This theme is expressed in Surah As-Sajdah:

'And if We had willed, We could have given every soul its guidance,' (As-Sajdah: 13).

That is: if We had willed to force people to believe, We could have made everyone a believer; but We have tested people by giving them choice—seeing who chooses the path of faith and who of disbelief; so those who choose the path of disbelief, We will fill Hell with them, and those who believe, We will admit them to Paradise.

This same point is stated with great clarity in Surah Ad-Dahr as follows:

'Indeed, this is a reminder, so he who wills may take to his Lord a way. And you do not will except that Allah wills. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise. He admits whom He wills into His mercy; but the wrongdoers - He has prepared for them a painful punishment.' (Ad-Dahr: 29-31).

In this verse, the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions are consoled—stating: do not be worried by people's stubbornness and defiance. This Qur'an is not a thing to be forced upon people. This is only a reminder, so use it to remind people. Whoever wills may believe, and whoever wills may choose the path of disbelief. If you desire their faith, nothing can happen from your desire until the willing of Allah is there, and the willing of Allah is based on His knowledge and His wisdom. He admits into His mercy only those whom He pleases, and He pleases only those who do not wrong their own souls, but value the capabilities He has blessed them with and appreciate His guidance. As for those who walk like the blind despite having eyes, He has prepared a painful punishment for such wrongdoers.

"Or have they taken protectors besides Him? But Allah - He is the Protector, and He gives life to the dead, and He is over all things competent." (9)

The question here is in the sense of expressing wonder and negation. In the preceding verse, it was stated that they will have neither any protector nor helper. This is a further explanation—stating: if they have taken other protectors besides Allah, this is their own assumption. The true Protector is Allah alone—in this world as well as in the Hereafter. He alone will raise the dead alive, and the presentation of everyone is before Him; so how can anyone else become a protector? and He is competent over everything; so in His presence, what need remains for any other protector, and what protection of any other protector can be of any use before Him?

"And in anything over which you disagree - its ruling is [referred] to Allah. [Say], 'That is Allah, my Lord; upon Him I have relied, and to Him I turn.'" (10)

Leaving the Matter of Those Who are Persistent on Their Stubbornness to Allah

In verse 6 above, the Prophet (pbuh) was consoled—stating: do not be worried by the opponents' stubbornness; the Sunnah He has appointed regarding guidance and deviation is inevitable, and these people are caught under the law of God; because of this, leave their matter to Allah. In compliance with this instruction, the Prophet (pbuh) referred their matter to Allah. Since this was in compliance with the preceding instruction, there was no need for any new instruction from Allah Almighty, but the words were directly declared through the tongue of the Prophet (pbuh). He said: 'In whatever matter of the religion sent by Allah you disagreed—whether Tawhid or the Hereafter—my responsibility was only to deliver the truth, and I have delivered it to you. Now, its decision is with Allah. He alone will decide whether I showed any negligence in delivering the truth or if you recognized and denied it! He alone is my Lord; because of this, I have relied on Him, and to Him I turn to judge between me and you.'

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 11–20

First, the same theme of Tawhid is explained further which has been mentioned from above—showing that the true Protector is Allah alone. Sustenance and children are His gifts, and the harmony among the opposites in this universe is clear proof that no other will besides the One Allah is operating within it.

After this, the first verse's theme is explained further—showing that all prophets from Hazrat Nuh to Hazrat Masih (peace be upon them) taught this very religion of Tawhid, and forbade people from creating differences in it; but their nations, despite the knowledge that came to them from God, created differences in it and deprived themselves of His guidance.

Next, the Prophet (pbuh) is instructed: invite the people to this very religion of the prophets, and remain firm upon your invitation. Warn those who are opposing you: 'I believe in the Book of Allah which He has sent down as a scale of justice to decide your differences. If you do not accept its decision, the scale of the Resurrection will decide your affairs, and the Resurrection is inevitable.' Do not assume your present comfort to be a proof of your being upon the truth. Allah gives the blessings of the world even to the enemies of the truth, but they will have no share in the Hereafter. Recite the verses.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"[He is] Creator of the heavens and the earth. He has made for you from yourselves mates, and among the cattle, mates; He multiplies you therein. There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the Hearing, the Seeing." (11)

Proof of Allah's Oneness

This is a further explanation of 'That is Allah, my Lord; upon Him I have relied' which passed in verse 10—showing that He alone is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, and He Who brought all humans and other creations into existence. There is no other who is His equal in His essence or attributes; so how can anyone else be deserving of being made His partner, protector, and place of return!

In 'He multiplies you therein' (yadhra'ukum feehi), the referent of the pronoun is not present in words, but is in the meaning understood from the words. From the words, it is understood: Allah Almighty, by creating mates for humans and cattle from their own kind, has made a farm or field within their own system of kind, in which He continuously multiplies and nurtures them. In the Arabic language, pronouns are brought in this manner. Some highly clear examples of this have passed in this book.

'There is nothing like unto Him'—this is the result of that power and wisdom of the Creator of the heavens and the earth which was pointed to: He Who created such great things as the heavens and the earth, and Who established this wonderful system to run the progeny of humans and cattle, who else can be such as to be declared His equal? There is nothing like Him, nor can there be. He is entirely One in His essence and attributes. Then from this, the result emerges: when He alone is the Creator of the heavens, then whatever descends from them is sent down by Him alone; when He alone is the Maker of the earth, then whatever emerges from it is necessarily brought forth by Him alone. When He alone is the Creator of male and female, then the creation that comes into existence from them is also brought into existence by Him alone. The meaning is: when the creation of the heavens, the earth, male, and female cannot be attributed to anyone besides God, how can the things coming into existence from their mutual interaction be attributed to anyone else? Who is there who has performed any of these works which God has performed, such that he could be made His equal?

'and He is the Hearing, the Seeing'—this is the subsequent lesson from the above: just as from the observation of this universe, the reality emerges that no one is His equal, similarly, this reality also emerges: the Hearing, the Seeing is He alone. Only a Hearing, Seeing Creator can bring such a vast universe into existence, and only He can maintain it... so when His power is unparalleled and His knowledge is all-encompassing, everyone must worship Him alone, and ask their needs of Him alone. He hears and knows the words of everyone. No medium is required to ask of Him.

"To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth. He extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it]. Indeed, He is, of all things, Knowing." (12)

He Who is the Creator is also the Owner

When Allah Almighty is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, it is also necessary that He alone is their Owner. How is it possible that He creates them but hands over the keys to their depositories to others? By His command the rain falls, by His command the earth brings forth its treasures, and He alone, in accordance with His knowledge and His wisdom, extends provision for whom He pleases and restricts it for whom He pleases. Neither is there any share of any deity in the creation of provision, nor in its distribution. All these matters are in the control of Allah Almighty; therefore, He alone is the true deserving recipient of gratitude. He is Knowing of everything; therefore, the servants must hope in Him alone and fear Him alone, and if anyone faces restriction of provision, they should not fall into the bad thought that God is unaware of it or has committed any injustice against them, but should remain assured that whatever is happening is in the knowledge of God, and in it lies His wisdom.

"He has ordained for you of religion what He enjoined upon Noah and that which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what We enjoined upon Abraham and Moses and Jesus - to establish the religion and not be divided therein. Difficult upon the polytheists is that to which you invite them. Allah chooses for Himself whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns [to Him]." (13)

This Religion is the Shared Religion of All Prophets

In the introduction of the Surah, addressing the Prophet (pbuh), it was stated that you are being revealed that very same religion of truth which was revealed to the prophets and messengers who came before you; this explains this very same theme addressing the Quraysh and the Arabs—showing that Allah has ordained for you the very same religion which He gave to Nuh, and which is being revealed to Muhammad (pbuh). This very religion was enjoined upon Ibrahim, Musa, and Isa (peace be upon them). This is no unique and strange religion, but the religion of everyone from the beginning until this final Prophet, and this alone is the true religion of Allah.

The mention of the Prophets (peace be upon them) here is such that first, the initial and final links, i.e., Hazrat Nuh and the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad (pbuh), are mentioned. Then, among the intermediate prophets, three glorious prophets—Hazrat Ibrahim, Hazrat Musa, and Hazrat Isa (peace be upon them)—are mentioned by name. The reason for specifically mentioning them with this care is that the claimants of following these three prophets were present before the Qur'an. The Arab polytheists claimed to follow Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), and the Jews and Christians, respectively, Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him). In this way, an allusion was made to the entire history of the prophets, and the prominent nations were also addressed.

The Instruction to the Nations regarding This Religion

'to establish the religion and not be divided therein'—this describes the target of the covenant which was enjoined upon these prophets, and the instruction given to their followers through these prophets. The definite article on 'Ad-Deen' is like that on 'Al-Kitab'—just as 'Al-Kitab' means the Book of Allah, 'Ad-Deen' means the religion of Allah. The religion of Allah has been Islam from the beginning, as He stated: 'Indeed, the religion with Allah is Islam.' The foundation of this religion is on pure and perfect Tawhid. This was given by Allah Almighty to Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him), rather to Hazrat Adam (peace be upon him), and this was sent down upon Muhammad (pbuh). Its beliefs and foundations have been identical from beginning to end; if any difference occurred, it occurred in the details of the Shariah, which the Qur'an has expressed with the words 'Shari'atan wa minhaja'.

The instruction was also given to the nations regarding this religion that they establish it and do not create differences and division in it. This is like the instruction: 'And hold firmly to the rope of Allah all together and do not become divided' (Al-Imran: 103). Establishing it means that its matters of belief are accepted with truth, and its duties of deeds are performed with honesty and uprightness. Also, people are continuously supervised so that they do not become negligent of or deviate from it, and care is taken that innovators do not create any rift in it.

'do not become divided' means this religion is the rope of Allah; therefore, everyone must hold it together, and it should not be that whosoever gets any rope in their hand, they consider it the rope of Allah and leave this rope. If connection with this rope is cut, the entire structure will collapse, and nothing else will be able to organize the people.

The Specific Thing with Which the Polytheists Had Conflict

'Difficult upon the polytheists is that to which you invite them'—'polytheists' refers to the polytheists of the Quraysh. It states: Allah has ordained for them the very same religion which He ordained for all prophets, and which was taught by their ancestor Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), but the thing is highly painful for them to which you invite them.

Although 'that to which you invite them' (ma tad'oohum ilayh) is general in words, the context is proof that it refers to Tawhid. In the Qur'an, it is repeatedly clarified that the polytheists had the greatest conflict with the Qur'an's invitation to Tawhid.

The Divine Law regarding Guidance

'Allah chooses for Himself whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns [to Him].' This consoled the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: observe patience over their state; no matter how much you desire their faith, only what Allah wills will happen, and His willing is based on His wisdom and His Sunnah. He guides to Him only those who turn to Him. Those who do not turn to Him remain deprived of His guidance. After 'yajtabee' (chooses), the preposition 'ila' is an indication of an encompassed meaning. Its explanation has passed.

"And they did not become divided until after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord [postponing punishment] until a specified term, it would have been concluded between them. And indeed, those who were granted inheritance of the Scripture after them are, concerning it, in disquieting doubt." (14)

Reproaching the Nations

This is a reproach to those nations—showing that after receiving the light of divine knowledge, they disputed with one another purely out of stubbornness and fell into deviation. If a person stumbles in the darkness of the night, they can be considered excused to some extent, but the one who stumbles in the full light of day, purely out of stubbornness, is responsible for his own foolishness. To understand the nature of this division, this indication will probably be sufficient: differences arose among the Jewish scholars and priests exactly as differences have arisen among us. The only difference is that this Ummah has the Qur'an protected; therefore, the touchstone to remove differences is present; but the Jews lost the Torah as well; because of this, no way of removing their differences remained.

The nation which appeared through the invitation of Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) and the Jews had a conflict from the very beginning, and the primary factor in this was the malice of the Jewish scholars. They stood up as their enemies purely out of stubbornness and envy. Later, many factions were created among the Christians themselves, and Paul deprived them of the light of both the Torah and the Gospel. After this, when Allah Almighty wanted to show them the light of the Qur'an, the Jews, the Christians, and the Quraysh combined to try to blow out this light, as you are reading in the Qur'an. The Jews had malice against the Qur'an because they did not want religious leadership to be transferred to the Children of Ismail, and the causes of the Quraysh's conflict with the Prophet (pbuh) are clear from these Surahs. This situation is expressed by the Qur'an with the words: 'And they did not become divided until after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves'—showing that whichever nation adopted division and difference, adopted it purely due to their bad deeds and mutual malice. As far as Allah and His messenger are concerned, guidance to the straight path was continuously provided from them.

'And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord [postponing punishment] until a specified term, it would have been concluded between them'—this is a warning to those nations, and also consolation for the Prophet (pbuh)—showing that since it has been decided by your Lord that to establish proof, every nation will be granted respite until a specified term, they are being granted respite. If this had not been so, decisions would have been made between them immediately.

'And indeed, those who were granted inheritance of the Scripture after them are, concerning it, in disquieting doubt.' This clause relates to the preceding clause: 'And they did not become divided until after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves.' The intermediate clause is parenthetical, for warning. That is: after receiving knowledge from Allah, these nations created differences among themselves, which resulted in those who inherited the Scripture (the Torah) after them falling into disquieting doubt regarding it. This theme has passed in verse 45 of Surah Fussilat: 'And We certainly gave Moses the Scripture, but it was differed over. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them. And indeed they are, concerning it, in disquieting doubt.' 'Those who were granted inheritance of the Scripture' refers to the Jewish factions who inherited the Torah after their predecessors. Since the Torah was received by them in a highly conflicting form, their falling into doubts regarding it was natural, and this required that they valued the Book which was sent down to remove these differences, i.e., the Qur'an; but they opposed it purely out of stubbornness and envy. Generally, commentators have interpreted 'the Scripture' (Al-Kitab) as the Qur'an, but there is no context for this.

"So to that [religion of Allah] invite, [O Muhammad], and remain steadfast as you are commanded and do not follow their desires but say, 'I have believed in what Allah has sent down of the Book, and I have been commanded to do justice among you. Allah is our Lord and your Lord. For us are our deeds, and for you your deeds. There is no [need for] argument between us and you. Allah will bring us together, and to Him is the destination.'" (15)

Instruction of Steadfastness to the Prophet

This instructed the Prophet (pbuh): invite the people to this religion of truth which is the shared religion of all prophets, and no matter how much your opponents try, do not care about them, but remain steadfast upon it exactly as you have been commanded.

'and do not follow their desires'—this explains steadfastness from its negative aspect: do not follow their desires—meaning do not follow those innovations which they have introduced in the religion of Allah to make their desires their religion. 'Ahwa'' (desires) refers, as has been explained, to innovations, because innovations are produced entirely by desires.

'but say, "I have believed in what Allah has sent down of the Book"'—meaning tell those who are disputing with you in defense of their innovations: 'I believe in the Book sent down by Allah, so how can I follow your innovations and desires?'

'"and I have been commanded to do justice among you"'—meaning: 'I have not come to follow your innovations and desires, but to do justice among you. You have created disputes in the religion of Allah; I have been commanded to decide these disputes to show what is the truth and what is falsehood; so do not expect of me that I follow your desires, but if you have the capability, try to know from me with whom is the truth and who is upon falsehood, and what is the true and pure religion of Allah.'

'"Allah is our Lord and your Lord..."'—this is a warning: remember that Allah alone is our Lord and your Lord. Our presentation must be before Him, and your presentation must be before Him. Do not remain in the misconception that your protector or place of return will be someone else; and remember also that we will be responsible for our deeds, and you for your deeds. If we have delivered the truth, we have discharged our duty; now, your responsibility before God is not upon us, but upon yourselves. You yourselves will face its consequences. This theme has passed in Surah Yunus:

'And if they deny you, then say, "For me are my deeds, and for you are your deeds. You are disassociated from what I do, and I am disassociated from what you do."' (Yunus: 41).

'"There is no [need for] argument between us and you"'—meaning: after all this discussion, explanation, and detail, if the matter is still not understood by you, then there is no need for further discussion between us and you. Now, the matter is left to Allah; He will gather us and make His decision, and remember that everyone's destination is to Him.

"And those who argue concerning Allah after He has been responded to - their argument is invalid in the sight of their Lord, and upon them is wrath, and for them is a severe punishment." (16)

'Muhajjah' means to dispute and argue, and in 'concerning Allah' (fillah), a prefixed noun is omitted—meaning: concerning the Tawhid of Allah (fi tawhidillah), because the dispute was not regarding God, but regarding His Tawhid. The People of the Book and the Arab polytheists did not deny God. But believing in God with shirk is not accepted in religion; rather, it is necessary to believe in Him with His Tawhid; if anyone disputes regarding Tawhid, they are disputing regarding God and are negating Him.

'after He has been responded to' (min ba'di ma-stujeeba lahu) shares almost the same meaning as 'after knowledge had come to them'—meaning this dispute was raised after they had believed in God. Obviously, when the teachings of all prophets from Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) to Hazrat Masih (peace be upon him) have been one, and all taught Tawhid, it means that the ancestors of the Quraysh, Jews, and Christians accepted the Tawhid of Allah. Now, after this acceptance, if these disputes are being raised by these nations regarding the Tawhid of God, this is not argument, but kist-disputing, and this disputing is not going to be of any use before God, but will be defeated.

"It is Allah who has sent down the Book in truth and the balance. And what will make you perceive? Perhaps the Hour is near." (17)

The Qur'an is the Balance

'The Book' refers to the Qur'an, and 'the balance' (Al-Meezan) is its explanation. It states: to remove the mutual differences of the nations and to distinguish between truth and falsehood, Allah has sent down the Qur'an, which is in reality a balance of justice. Above in verse 15, through the tongue of the Prophet (pbuh), this declaration was made: 'I have believed in what Allah has sent down of the Book, and I have been commanded to do justice among you'; this same point is stated here in other words. Obviously, when the Prophet (pbuh) was commanded by Allah to decide the differences of the nations regarding the religion of Allah, it was necessary that you were given a Book in truth which serves as a balance of justice, in whose light you could evaluate how much truth and falsehood is with whom. Due to this characteristic of the Qur'an, its name is also 'Al-Muhaymin'—meaning the touchstone. This same theme has occurred in Surah Al-Hadid:

'and We sent down with them the Scripture and the balance that the people may maintain [their affairs] in justice...' (25).

'And what will make you perceive? Perhaps the Hour is near'—this phrase is highly comprehensive and highly eloquent here. The meaning is: fortunate will be those who decide their differences and distinguish between truth and falsehood in accordance with the decision of this balance of justice; otherwise, the Resurrection is bound to decide, and no one will be able to escape from its decision. And do not consider the Resurrection far; what wonder if it has drawn near. Those who are turning away from the Qur'an's balance of justice, where will they flee from the balance of the Resurrection!

"Those who do not believe in it are impatient for it, but those who believe are fearful of it and know that it is the truth. Unquestionably, those who dispute concerning the Hour are in extreme error." (18)

That is, these people consider the Resurrection to be merely a threat; because of this, they do not care about it, but say with audacity: 'If it has to come, why does it not come?' It states: those who do not believe in the Resurrection are impatient for it, as if it is merely a joke to them. But those who believe in it understand its terror and remain fearful of it.

'Unquestionably, those who dispute concerning the Hour are in extreme error'—this is an expression of regret over the misfortune of those who dispute regarding such an obvious reality as the Resurrection. It states: these people have fallen into extreme error, and their eyes will open when opening them will be of no use. Rather, the end which they are denying will be before everyone, and no one will have any escape from it.

"Allah is subtle [i.e., gentle] with His servants; He provides for whom He wills, and He is the Powerful, the Exalted in Might." (19)

The Wisdom in Granting Respite to the Rebellious

This describes the cause of why He grants such respite to the rebellious and wicked that they become bold to demand the Resurrection and the punishment. It states: Allah Almighty is highly gentle with His servants. He is ahead in showing mercy, and does not hasten in sending the punishment. Despite their rebellion, He continues to provide for them so that if they wish, they may benefit from this respite to repent and reform and become deserving of His eternal mercy.

'and He is the Powerful, the Exalted in Might'—the second reason is: God is highly Powerful and Exalted in Might. No one has the strength to escape His grasp; so when no one is outside His control, why would He hasten? Haste is shown by one who fears that the prey will escape from his grasp.

"Whoever desires the harvest of the Hereafter - We increase for him in his harvest. And whoever desires the harvest of this world - We give him thereof, but there is not for him in the Hereafter any share." (20)

This is a further explanation of that divine law—showing that the rule of Allah is not to provide only for those who perform His worship, but He provides for both the righteous and the wicked in this world; however, those who cultivate the harvest of the Hereafter, Allah increases their harvest, and also gives them of the provision of this world whatever He pleases. As for those who seek only this world and do not care about the Hereafter, Allah gives them of it whatever His wisdom requires. However, they will have no share in the Hereafter.

After 'We increase for him in his harvest' (nazid lahu fee harthihi), the words 'and We give him of the world' are omitted. The contrast is proof of this omission, because in the second clause, the words 'We give him thereof' (nu'tihi minha) are present. In Surah Al-Isra', this theme has passed in these words:

'Whoever should desire the immediate - We hasten for him from it what We will to whom We intend. Then We have made for him Hell, which he will [enter to] burn, censured and banished. But whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts for it its [due] exertion while he is a believer - those are the ones whose exertion is appreciated [by Allah]. To each [category] We extend - to these and to those - from the gift of your Lord. And never has the gift of your Lord been restricted.' (Al-Isra': 18-20).

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 21–36

In the preceding passage, the reality was clarified that all prophets and messengers taught the same religion of Tawhid which this Prophet (Muhammad [pbuh]) is teaching. Now, next, it is clarified: if their assumed partners have fabricated a different religion for them, it is not going to be of any use before God. On the Day of Judgment, these people will beat their heads over their misfortune. The success of that Day will be the share only of those who walk upon the path of faith and righteous deeds to cultivate for that Day.

After this, the three objections of the opponents are answered in sequence:

* First, to their objection: 'the Prophet is presenting this invitation with such zeal not because he cares about our welfare, but because his own interest is hidden in it.'

* Second, to their objection: 'this discourse he is presenting is entirely his own fabrication, but he is falsely attributing it to God to overawe us.'

* Third, to their objection: 'if we deserve punishment for our deviation, why are our conditions better than his and his companions' conditions, and why does that punishment of which he is warning us not come?'

Recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"Or have they partners who have ordained for them of religion what Allah has not permitted? But if not for the decisive word, it would have been concluded between them. And indeed, the wrongdoers will have a painful punishment." (21)

From Where Did This Religion of Shirk Come!

'Am' (Or) here is in the sense of expressing wonder and negation. The meaning is: the religion of Allah, which He sent through His prophets, is what has been described above, so from where did this new religion come! Do they have some partners who have fabricated for them a religion which Allah has not permitted? Obeying any religion without Allah's permission is not permissible, so from where did this religion come? If they have partners, Allah has no partner. This is a mere assumption of theirs.

'But if not for the decisive word, it would have been concluded between them'—this is a threat to them, and its parallel has passed in verse 14 above—stating: if Allah had not appointed a time for their decision, their affair would have been concluded in this world. So let them not be deceived by the respite they have received, but rather remain grateful to Allah that He is not hastening in seizing them. If they do not benefit from this respite, they must remember that for such wrongdoers is a painful punishment. 'Wrongdoers' (Zalimun) refers to the polytheists, who abandoned the religion sent by Allah to fabricate a new religion of shirk, and thus wronged their own souls.

"You will see the wrongdoers fearful of what they earned, and it will [certainly] befall them. And those who believe and do righteous deeds will be in the meadows of the Gardens. They will have whatever they desire with their Lord. That is the great bounty." (22)

In the Hereafter, a Person Will Face Their Deeds

That is, today these polytheists are carefree of the Hereafter, relying on their assumed partners and believing that if the Hereafter occurs, their partners will help them and they need not worry. But when the Resurrection occurs and they see that there is no partner or intercessor, but rather everyone has to face their own deeds, they will be fearful of the consequences of their deeds, but this fear of theirs will be entirely useless. The consequence of their deeds will necessarily fall upon them, and they will not be able to avert it in any way.

'And those who believe...'—on the contrary, those who, instead of relying on deities, led a life of faith and righteous deeds will be in the meadows of the Gardens. They will have whatever they desire with their Lord.

In 'the meadows of the Gardens' (rowdatil-jannat), 'rowdat' refers to those meadows and lawns constructed in the vast Paradise of Allah for the recreation of the people of Paradise. Meadows will be numerous and gardens will be numerous; for this reason, both are in the plural.

'That is the great bounty'—to understand the force of this clause, have a look at verse 20 above.

It was stated there that the seekers of this world consider these broken pieces of clay as a highly great thing, whereas the true favor and bounty, which people should seek, is this which Allah will bestow upon His believing servants on the Day of Judgment.

"It is that of which Allah gives glad tidings to His servants who believe and do righteous deeds. Say, [O Muhammad], 'I do not ask you for it any payment except love among kinsmen.' And whoever commits a good deed - We will increase for him good therein. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Appreciative." (23)

This explains the greatness of this great bounty—stating that Allah is giving His believing servants the glad tidings of an eternal kingdom; so they should strive for it and leave those who are infatuated with the broken pieces of clay of this world.

'Say, [O Muhammad], "I do not ask you for it any payment except love among kinsmen."'—the exception is, in my view, disconnected (munqati'), and 'qurba' (kinsmen) is in the sense of a verbal noun (masdar), like 'zulfa' and 'bushra'.

Warning to the Leaders of the Quraysh

The meaning is: tell these proud leaders of the Quraysh: 'I am dedicated to your guidance day and night despite all your disregard and offenses, so do not assume that some personal interest of mine is hidden in this, for which He has promised me that great bounty which was mentioned above; how can He Who promised me that great bounty ask any payment of you! All my efforts and anxieties are because I want to discharge that right of kinship and relationship which is between me and you. You are my family and my nation; therefore, it is my duty to inform you of the guidance and warning I have brought from God, and try to make you partners in this mercy of which I am inviting.'

An Attribute of the Honorable Prophets

It should be kept in mind here that it has always been the Sunnah of the honorable Prophets that they initiated their invitation from their family and tribe, and as long as they did not despair of them, they did not turn toward others. In accordance with this Sunnah, the Prophet (pbuh) was also instructed at the beginning: 'And warn your closest kindred' (Al-Shu'ara': 214). Your closest kindred were the Quraysh, who enjoyed the religious and political leadership of the entire Arabia. You warned them first, and despite all their offenses, you continued with this work until they despaired you. Finally, when the situation reached the extent that they conferred to kill and turn you out, you migrated to Medina by the command of Allah Almighty.

The Perspective of the Mentor-Scholar (ra)

The mentor-scholar (ra) looks at this verse from a slightly different angle: he considers this exception in the sense of 'istidrak' (however), and interprets the verse in the light of verse 47 of Surah Saba: 'Say, "Whatever payment I might have asked of you - it is yours. My payment is only from Allah."' In his view, the meaning is: 'My inviting you to maintain family ties, perform duties, and spend is not because of any personal interest, but is for your own benefit in this world and the Hereafter. This wealth will be taken from your rich and distributed among your poor, and in this way, I am showing you the way of maintaining family ties. There is no personal interest of mine in this.'

It should be kept in mind here that the Prophet (pbuh) did not make the wealth of Zakah permissible for himself and his relatives, so that there remained no room for any such doubt.

There is no great distance between these two interpretations, as both 'inqita' (disconnection) and 'istidrak' are of the same category. However, there is some difference. While I am satisfied with my interpretation, I do not declare the interpretation of the mentor-scholar (ra) to be incorrect.

The Perspective of the Author

In my view, the correct interpretation of the verse is what has been explained above. Generally, people have interpreted its meaning as: 'I do not ask you any payment, but I want that due to the kinship between me and you, you maintain a good relationship with me.' The weakness in this interpretation is entirely clear: asking for a good relationship is also demanding a payment, and in Surah Ash-Shu'ara', it is quoted from all prophets—Nuh, Hud, Salih, Lut, and Shu'ayb (peace be upon them): 'I do not ask of you for it any payment. My payment is only from the Lord of the worlds.' No prophet ever appealed to his nation for a good relationship in exchange for his invitation, because all the dispute was due to the invitation; so when there was no room for any compromise on the part of the prophet, what was the sense in appealing for a good relationship! The Quraysh's malice against the Prophet (pbuh) was purely due to his invitation. They were ready to fulfill his every desire and comply with his every command provided he desisted from his invitation to Tawhid, but you clearly stated: 'I cannot desist from this invitation.' Following this, when the Quraysh's attitude became more severe, instead of asking them for a good relationship, you clearly warned them: 'Do whatever you please; even if I am given gold equal to Mount Uhud, I am not going to desist from my invitation.'

Some deviated factions have interpreted its meaning as: 'I only ask this payment for this invitation: that you love my relatives.' This interpretation is contrary to the words of the verse, other texts, and reason. But once the correct interpretation is clarified, there remains no need to refute it. Those who made this interpretation did not even remember that this verse is of the Meccan period when your close relatives were mostly your mortal enemies; what was the sense in asking people to love them!

A Further Warning to the Quraysh

'And whoever commits a good deed - We will increase for him good therein. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Appreciative'—meaning tell these people: 'Allah is not in need of your goodness and piety, such that if you do not perform His worship, some rift will appear in His divinity; rather, in it lies your own benefit, because whatever small or great virtue you perform, Allah will increase it, and you will receive its reward in the form of an eternal kingdom. Allah is Forgiving and Appreciative of His servants' virtues. He will not ignore even the smallest virtue, but will nurture it to increase it and bestow it upon His servant as a great reward.'

"Or do they say, 'He has invented about Allah a lie'? But if Allah willed, He could seal over your heart. And Allah eliminates falsehood and establishes the truth by His words. Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts." (24)

An Expression of Actual Reality

That is, if against this balance of justice (the Qur'an) they have raised this fitnah—claiming that it is your own invention but you have falsely attributed it to God to overawe people, then for your satisfaction, this is sufficient: you have neither achieved this of your own desire, nor can you achieve it; but God Himself has caused this spring of favor to flow within you, and He can stop it whenever He wills. This same theme has passed in Surah Al-Isra' as follows:

'And if We willed, We could surely, do away with that which We revealed to you. Then you would not find for yourself concerning it an advocate against Us. Except as a mercy from your Lord. Indeed, His favor upon you has ever been great.' (Al-Isra': 86-87).

In 'And Allah eliminates falsehood and establishes the truth by His words', 'wa' (and) is for state (hal), and 'yamhu' (eliminates) is not conjoined with 'yakhtim' (seal), but this is the subsequent sentence. This is originally 'yamhoo', but in the script of the manuscripts, the final 'wa' has been dropped in some places, such as 'yad'ul-insan' and 'sanad'uz-zabaniyah', etc.

Another Aspect of the Reality

This is another aspect of the reality—asking: do those who declare this Qur'an to be a fabrication not see its effect—that Allah is eliminating falsehood and establishing the truth through His words? A fabrication is always from Satan, and it is not the work of Satan to eliminate falsehood and establish the truth; rather, his work is entirely opposite to it. Some similar words were quoted from Lord Masih in reply to those who accused him of driving out devils with the help of Beelzebub, the chief of the devils—showing: 'If I drive out devils with the help of Satan, it means Satan has stood up against himself.' This same point is stated here: if this discourse is a fabrication and its presenter a fabricator, its effect would not be the establishment of truth and the elimination of falsehood, but rather entirely opposite to it. Which fabricator in the world has presented such a blessed and enlightening discourse as this Qur'an!

Warning to the Opponents

'Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts'—this is a warning to those who were declaring the Qur'an to be a fabrication and the Prophet (pbuh) a fabricator. Obviously, those who raised this fitnah knew very well that the Qur'an is not a fabrication and its presenter is not a fabricator. They were well aware of the grandeur of the Qur'an and the moral greatness of the Prophet (pbuh). They knew very well that the Qur'an is the truth and its presenter is a person of stainless character; but since they were not ready to accept the Qur'an as the Book of Allah and the Prophet as His messenger, contrary to the voice of their own conscience, they fabricated such statements. Pointing to this, He threatened: 'God is well aware of the secrets of your hearts—of what is in your hearts, and what you are saying with your tongues!'

Encouraging Repentance and Reform

"And He it is who accepts repentance from His servants and pardons misdeeds and knows what you do." (25)

This is to encourage them to repent and reform—showing that the opportunity still remains: if you wish, you can save yourselves from the wrath of God through repentance and reform. Allah Almighty is highly Merciful and Forgiving of His servants' sins. Otherwise, remember that He is fully aware of what you do. You cannot hide any of your crimes from Him.

"And He answers [the supplication of] those who have believed and done righteous deeds and increases for them from His bounty. And the disbelievers will have a severe punishment." (26)

This places the way of the believers and their reward before them—showing that just as they were granted the capability to accept this invitation, as a reward for which they will receive His further favors in this world and the Hereafter, similarly, these people can also make themselves deserving of these favors if they wish; otherwise, they must remember that for the disbelievers is a severe punishment. In my view, 'those who have believed' (alladhina amanoo) is in the nominative case as the subject (fa'il), not as the object (maf'ul). In another place, He stated: 'Only those will respond who listen. And the dead - Allah will resurrect them...' (Al-An'am: 36).

"And if Allah had extended provision for His servants, they would have committed tyranny on earth. But He sends down in a [due] measure what He wills. Indeed, He is, of His servants, Acquainted and Seeing." (27)

Correcting a Misconception

That is, if these people assume their worldly wealth and tharwat to be a proof of their being upon the truth, and in this pride do not care about the Prophet (pbuh) and his poor companions, this is a mere self-deception. Worldly wealth is no proof of being upon the truth. This is merely for trial, and Allah gives this to both disbelievers and believers according to His wisdom, and tests both. This is entirely in the control of Allah. He is fully aware of the states and interests of His servants; because of this, under His wisdom, He extends for whomever He pleases and restricts for whomever He pleases, and thus tests their patience or gratitude. If He had willed, He could have extended His provisions for everyone to take as much as they pleased, but this would have become a great fitnah for them, and people would have set out on the path of rebellion and tyranny in the intoxication of wealth. To protect people from this fitnah, Allah has kept this matter entirely in His own control. He sends down in a due measure what He wills. If He has given more to someone, let him not fall into the pride: 'this is the miracle of my planning and capability, or my right and my ancestors' right'; but let him remain grateful to his Lord in Whose control is bestowing and also snatching.

This theme has passed with greater clarity in Surah Az-Zukhruf, which is its counterpart:

'And they said, "Why was this Qur'an not sent down upon a great man from [one of] the two cities [i.e., Makkah and Ta'if]?" Do they distribute the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have apportioned among them their livelihood in the life of this world and have raised some of them above others in degrees [of rank] that they may take one another in service. But the mercy of your Lord is better than what they accumulate. And if it were not that the people would become one nation [of disbelievers], We would have made for those who disbelieve in the Most Merciful - for their houses - ceilings of silver and stairways upon which they mount. And for their houses - doors and thrones upon which they recline, And gold ornament. But all that is not but the enjoyment of worldly life. And the Hereafter with your Lord is for the righteous.' (31-35).

"And He it is who sends down the rain after they had despaired and spreads His mercy. And He is the Protector, the Praiseworthy." (28)

Proof for the Preceding Statement

This is presented as proof for the preceding statement—showing that He alone sends down the provision for His servants in a due measure, and neither do the servants have any choice in this, nor do their assumed partners. It states: He alone sends down the rain after they have despaired. Rain is mentioned specifically because livelihood depends entirely upon it. And 'after they had despaired' (min ba'di ma qanatoo) is meant to point to the reality: all those who are proud of their planning, their entitlement, their resources, and their partners, realize well that opening the closed gates of the sky is beyond the power of anyone but its Creator. The meaning is: Allah Almighty shows this glory of His from time to time so that no one falls into the pride that provision is anyone's inheritance, or that anyone besides Allah Almighty can be its source.

'And He is the Protector, the Praiseworthy'—this places the summary: this daily observation is proof that the true Protector is Allah alone, and He is Praiseworthy—meaning characterized by all attributes of praise and the source of all praise. In this temporary life, He does not keep even those deprived of His provision who live on His provision and challenge Him.

"And of His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and what He has dispersed throughout them of creatures. And He, for gathering them when He wills, is competent." (29)

Correcting Another Misconception

That is, let no one also remain in the misconception that the Resurrection or punishment of which they are being warned is a mere threat; the God Who created the heavens and the earth is, to a greater degree, competent to recreate people. This is the very same proof which is expressed in another place as: 'Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it.' (An-Nazi'at: 27).

The word 'da'bbah' is used for the living creatures of the earth; because of this, the question might arise: do such creatures also exist in the heavens, for which the dual pronoun is used in 'dispersed throughout them' (batha feehima)? The answer is: 'sama'' (heaven) is used for the sky and also for the atmosphere in which birds fly.

In this verse, the contrast of 'batha' (dispersed) and 'jam'' (gathering) is highly eloquent and a highly heart-touching proof of the Resurrection. 'Batha' means to disperse and spread, and 'jam'' means to gather. This hinted: the One Who dispersed these living creatures in the earth and atmosphere is competent to gather them whenever He wills. When He was competent to disperse, why would He be unable to gather? The farmer who sows seeds in his field does not sow them to destroy them, but gathers his harvest one day, and faces no difficulty in this.

"And whatever strikes you of disaster - it is for what your hands have earned; but He pardons much. And you will not escape on earth, and you have not besides Allah any protector or helper." (30-31)

Answering the Doubt from Another Aspect

This answers the doubt from another aspect. It states: you can experience the law of retribution of God in your daily life as well. The afflictions that touch you in this world are the results of your own deeds, though He pardons many bad deeds. These afflictions touch you for your warning and alerting so that you do not consider this world to be a plaything and lead a life of indifference in it, but receive this lesson from these warning events: its Creator is the One Who rewards and punishes, and He will necessarily gather you to call you to account one day. If He is granting you respite today, the cause is not that He has no concern with your good and evil, or has given your evil the status of good; rather, the cause is: He has appointed a specific Day of Decision for perfect reward and punishment, which is bound to come.

In 'and He pardons much' (wa ya'foo 'an katheer), 'afw' is in the sense of pardoning and overlooking. Its usage in this sense has occurred in various places.

'And you will not escape on earth'—meaning you observe with your eyes that when Allah Almighty sends His affliction in the earth, you cannot escape from His control, nor does any protector of yours stand in your defense. This is proof: in the same way in the heaven, on the Day of Judgment, neither will any partner save you from the grasp of God, nor any protector and helper.

It should be kept in mind here that the address is to the disbelievers, whom the afflictions touch as a retribution for their deeds. This shows that the Creator rewards and punishes; therefore, He will necessarily bring a Day of Recompense in which He will punish those crimes of theirs which He is overlooking in this world. This verse does not relate to the prophets and the righteous. The afflictions they face are not as a punishment for their deeds, but as a trial to test their patience, and this test becomes a source of the elevation of their ranks.

"And of His signs are the ships in the sea, like mountains. If He wills, He calms the wind, and they remain motionless on its back. Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful. Or He could destroy them for what they earned; but He pardons much. And [that] those who dispute concerning Our signs may know that for them there is no place of escape." (32-35)

A Parable for the Arrogant of Worldly Success

This presents a parable for those who are proud of their worldly successes—showing: consider this worldly life as a sea journey. It is of the miracle of Allah's power and wisdom that massive ships like mountains sail upon the chest of the sea. Those who do not look at God's power, when they see the ship sailing, fall into the pride: 'Where and how can any danger be faced by it?', whereas if He should will, He could calm the wind and these ships would remain motionless on the back of the sea along with their passengers, or He could destroy them along with their passengers. Although in this parable, sailing ships are meant, because only sailing ships were present at that time, exactly this same parable is applicable to today's advanced ships. Today, the developments of science have taught man to control steam, electricity, and atom, which has increased his power greatly, but today also we read regarding massive ships in newspapers: when Allah Almighty wills, they sink like paper, or all their machinery becomes paralyzed in such a way that they remain motionless like a dead body...

'Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful' points to that lesson which is hidden in this parable. It states: in this, there are great warnings for those who observe patience and gratitude. That is: those who reflect on the situations of life seriously derive this conclusion: if a person faces unfavorable conditions, he must not despair, but observe patience relying on his Lord. Whatever happens to man in this world happens by the command of God, and His every action is based on wisdom. In the same way, if a person receives favorable conditions, he must not become arrogant, but remain grateful to his Lord, because whatever a person receives, he receives from God, and He Who is competent to give is also competent to snatch. Those who are unaware of this reality are made arrogant by favor and despaired by adversity; they never become acquainted with the sweetness of faith. Below in this Surah, such people are mentioned in these words: 'And indeed, when We let man taste mercy from Us, he rejoices in it; but if evil strikes them for what their hands have put forth, then indeed, man is an ingrate.' (48).

'Or He could destroy them for what they earned; but He pardons much' is conjoined with 'If He wills, He calms the wind'. The parenthetical clause 'Indeed in that...' occurred in the middle to draw attention to the objective of the parable. After the parenthetical clause, the statement is completed—showing: just as He is competent to calm the wind and make the ships motionless, He is competent to destroy them along with their passengers as a retribution for their deeds, and He can pardon many of their sins so that they escape and reach the shore in safety. All three are in His control; for this reason, in this world, it is not permissible for anyone to become arrogant over any success, but they should remain grateful to their Lord upon achieving success, and if any adversity is faced, attribute it to their own shortcoming, observe patience, and hope for good from Allah.

An Example of Omission

'And [that] those who dispute concerning Our signs may know that for them there is no place of escape' (wa ya'lama-lladhina yujadiloona)—here, 'ya'lama' (know) is in the subjunctive case; therefore, we must assume a conjoined clause omitted. Precedents of this have passed and will come, for example: 'And Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth and that every soul may be recompensed for what it earned...' (Al-Jathiyah: 22). On this principle here, some suitable clause must be assumed. The complete statement is as if: 'that He may take retribution from them, and that on those who dispute concerning Allah's signs, this reality is made clear: there is no place of escape for them from God.'

This theme has occurred in the Qur'an in various places in different styles. We present a parallel from Surah Yunus:

'He it is who enables you to travel on land and sea...'

"So whatever thing you have been given is but enjoyment of worldly life, but what is with Allah is better and more lasting for those who have believed and upon their Lord rely." (36)

Summary of the Discussion

This places the summary of the discussion before us—stating that these blessings you have received in the world are not things over which to boast, but are the enjoyment of this temporary life. One day, this life and all its provisions will end. However, what is with Allah in the Hereafter is far better and eternal, which is the share of those who believed and rely upon their Lord. The meaning is: if you have ambition, seek this. Why do you ruin your Hereafter for the sake of this temporary enjoyment of the world.

Here, 'and upon their Lord rely' (wa 'ala Rabbihim yatawakkaloon) is highly significant. This points to the reality: for those who seek the eternal kingdom of the Hereafter, the primary provision in this path is trust (tawakkul). Unless a person gathers the courage to divorce those things of the world that obstruct the path of God, relying on His support and the reward of the Hereafter, they can never achieve this eternal kingdom.

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 37–43

Next, some other attributes of those deserving of the aforementioned reward are described, from which it emerges that those Muslims who, during this period, were being targeted with persecution at the hands of the arrogant Quraysh, to the extent that they were forced to leave their homes, are indeed its true deserving recipients. This Surah was revealed close to migration; because of this, the style of the description of these attributes is such as to bless the believers with victory, and also to guide them with some essential instructions regarding their duties in the subsequent stages—as if before migration, they were told that previously they were scattered individuals, but now Allah Almighty is going to organize them in a collective and political entity. Until now they were oppressed, but now the time is coming when they will achieve the power of defense and will be authorized to do so by Allah... what attitude they must adopt in these subsequent situations is guided in these verses, but this guidance is not in the form of command, but of describing the requirements of faith and trust... Recite the verses.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"And those who avoid the major sins and immoralities, and when they are angry, they forgive," (37)

Three Categories of Evils

That is: these people are not like those who fell into injustice, immorality, and rebellion upon receiving God's blessings; but they avoid all major crimes of injustice, immorality, and anger. Within this style of expression, the satire on the opponents is prominent, and if you reflect, all evils are summarized here under three categories: injustice (ithm), immorality (fahsha'), and anger (ghadhab)... 'Ithm' refers to those evils which are of the nature of injustice, unfairness, and tyranny. 'Fahsha'' refers to those evils which are produced by passions and desires of the self. 'Ghadhab' (anger) is produced by pride, arrogance, and self-will, and gives birth to tyranny, violence, and rebellion.

Here, only avoiding 'major' (kaba'ir) sins is mentioned. This has two reasons. First, man has been placed in a test within the conflict of the opposing urges of good and evil; because of this, Allah Almighty's demand of him is not to lead a completely sinless life. If this had been demanded, it would have been unbearable. For this reason, Allah Almighty has desired only that he tries to avoid major sins. If he avoids major evils, Allah Almighty will overlook his small lapses.

Second, the correct way to avoid small evils is also that a person avoids major evils. The one who performs major trusts, his conscience never agrees that he commits treachery in minor trusts. Similarly, the servant of Allah who protects himself from major evils does not want to ruin his reward by committing minor evils. The one who avoids stealing gold coins will not steal pence. If any such action is committed by him, it will be due to oversight, not deliberately. However, those who filter out a gnat are often seen swallowing a camel.

From 'and when they are angry, they forgive' (wa-idha ma ghadhiboo hum yaghfiroon), it emerges: as far as anger is concerned, they do feel it, because anger is a natural requirement of human non-com, honor, and self-respect; but they do not become out of control by this anger to suspend their intellect and cross God's limits; rather, they keep themselves under control and forgive those who deserve to be overlooked. Within this is also an instruction to the Muslims: though your enemies' conduct is highly provoking, it is still better to overlook them until proof is fully established against them. Further explanation is coming. By the first-person pronoun 'hum' (they), force is produced—showing that though this is highly difficult, highly deserving of appreciation are those who bear this.

"And those who have responded to their Lord and established prayer and whose affair is [determined by] consultation among themselves, and from what We have provided them, they spend." (38)

The Attributes from Which the Aforementioned Virtues are Produced

This describes those attributes from which those virtues mentioned in the preceding verse are produced. It states: they have responded to this invitation of their Lord which has reached them through His messenger. They did not try to deny it like the arrogant Quraysh. Their very virtue was pointed to in verse 26: 'And He answers [the supplication of] those who have believed and done righteous deeds and increases for them from His bounty.'

The Primary Manifestation of Faith is Prayer

'and established prayer' points to the primary manifestation of their faith—showing that they establish prayer. We have been clarifying in various places in this book: the first practical manifestation of faith is prayer. This is its first manifestation, and then from it, other virtues appear and are nurtured. For this reason, whoever does not establish prayer has not responded to the invitation of faith. If they claim to believe, this claim is mere self-deception.

Here, keep in view also that 'aqamoo-s-salat' (they established prayer) does not mean merely praying, but establishing it—meaning they pray and also arrange that others pray. This is the characteristic of their collective life and their primary distinction in this world. This prayer teaches them how they must stand like a solid structure to perform His worship and establish His religion, and this prayer protects them from that immorality and evil of which avoiding was mentioned in the preceding verse. This is pointed to in another place: 'Indeed, prayer prohibits immorality and wrongdoing' (Al-Ankabut: 45)... within this also is a satire on the leaders of the Quraysh: they destroyed the prayer, but still claim to be the inheritors of this House of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) which was constructed as a center of prayer.

The Political System of the Believers is Based on Consultation

'and whose affair is [determined by] consultation among themselves'—'Shura' is a verbal noun (masdar) on the scale of 'futya', and means to consult with one another. The word 'amr' in Arabic is used, like our word 'affair', in highly wide meanings. Its correct meaning is determined by the context. Here, the context indicates that it is used in the sense of collective system. That is, the collective and political system of the believers is not based on self-will, arrogance, family superiority, or lineage; but on the mutual consultation of the believers. Within this, the satire on the Quraysh's system is prominent, because their system was based entirely on lineage and family distinctions. Their opposition of Islam was largely because they saw the death of their ignorant system in its success.
