In this verse, the Qur'an blessed the Muslims with the glad tidings: the time has arrived for them to organize in a collective and political entity, and this system will be based on the mutual consultation of the believers instead of lineage; on the other hand, the leaders of the Quraysh were warned: 'You may dedicate all your power to oppose Islam, but through these poor and oppressed Muslims, that new system is coming which will overturn your corrupt system.'

A Specific Aspect of the Importance of Consultation

Here, there is no room to discuss the characteristics of the consultation system and its principles. We have discussed this in detail in an independent chapter of our book *Islamic State*. Those who seek details should read it. However, a question is worth considering: the well-known style of the Qur'an is to mention spending or zakah along with prayer, but here, contrary to this, consultation is mentioned between prayer and spending; what is that specific aspect of its importance due to which it has been placed by the side of prayer?

Islam's Collective System Was Organized in the Form of Prayer

In our view, the answer is: the spirit and actual mold of Islam's collective system have been preserved in prayer. Within it, Muslims are shown: how they must stand like a solid structure for His worship, how they must choose the most knowledgeable and pious among them for their leadership (Imamah), how they must obey this leader within the limits of the Shariah, and how the leader is bound not to command them to do anything contrary to any command of Allah and His messenger, and how even his poorest follower has the right to correct him if he commits a mistake—to the extent that even within prayer, if any minor lapse occurs in bowing, prostration, standing, or recitation, every person praying behind him is responsible to warn him, and the leader is bound to accept it if it is correct, and correct his mistake immediately. Thus, by organizing our entire collective system in the form of prayer, We were shown: 'follow this model in your political organization.' In this way, organize yourselves for establishing His religion, choose the most capable, knowledgeable, and pious among you for your leadership, obey him in all معروف, and if anything contrary to the Shariah is committed by him, try to warn him without fear of any criticism.

This is the connection between prayer and our political system, because of which the Qur'an, at the time when Muslims were about to form a collective entity, guided them to consultation (Shura), and by mentioning it by the side of prayer, highlighted its grandeur, and also clarified the nature of its formation—showing: what will be the state of the rights and duties of the leader and the followers, what kind of people will be suitable for its membership, what responsibilities they carry regarding the community, and before Whom is their primary responsibility.

The importance of consultation and its connection with prayer was the reason that during the Prophetic era and the period of the Righteous Caliphs, it was held in the mosque. Caliph Hazrat Umar Farooq used to announce the consultation with the words: 'Al-Salat Jami'ah'—meaning: 'the believers should gather for prayer.' When they gathered, they performed two units of prayer. Obviously, when he prayed, the members of the consultation prayed behind him. After prayer and supplication, Hazrat Umar presented the issue under discussion, and they expressed their opinions to reach a consensus under the guidance of the Caliph.

This method is highly blessed in terms of achieving God's guidance, and also in terms of protecting the actual spirit of Islam's political system. But in this era, Muslims, in copying other nations, chose other systems, and their connection with mosques was completely cut off. Consequently, they were deprived of God's guidance, and their reins went into the hands of Satan and his agents. Now, the bickering that occurs in parliaments can barely be found even in markets.

'and from what We have provided them, they spend'—the connection of spending or zakah with prayer has been discussed in detail in various places in this book. One pillar of His worship is to pray, and the second is to spend in His way. The first establishes the servant's relation with his Creator; the second connects him with creation, and upon both of these foundations is the entire Shariah established.

"And those who, when tyranny strikes them, they defend themselves." (39)

What was stated in verse 37: 'and when they are angry, they forgive' is followed by a clarification—showing: first, they overlook the provoking conduct of the opponents, and if they take any defensive action, they take it when open tyranny is committed against them. The meaning is: the right of defense and retribution against any tyranny is possessed by every person, and they also benefit from this right, but they do not make every matter an excuse for action; rather, they avenge only 'tyranny' (baghy). 'Baghy' refers to any open transgression, rebellion, and violence of the opponent.

"And the retribution for an evil is an evil like thereof; but whoever pardons and makes reconciliation - his reward is upon Allah. Indeed, He does not like wrongdoers." (40)

Balance in Retribution

That is, when they avenge, they do not try to answer stones with larger stones; rather, they maintain full balance in this matter, taking action only equivalent to the evil.

Taking action of retribution is no evil, but is Qisas; but here, it is expressed with the word 'evil' (sayyi'ah) in accordance with that style of the Arabic language which has been explained—showing that sometimes words are used on the principle of homogeneity (mujanasat). For example: 'We dealt with them as they dealt'—obviously, 'as they dealt' means 'as they did', but for the sake of harmony, it is used in this manner.

It should be kept in mind here that even in taking retribution against someone, committing any action which is forbidden in the Shariah is not permissible.

In Individual Matters, the Desirable Way is Reform

'but whoever pardons and makes reconciliation - his reward is upon Allah'—meaning: although every person possesses the right to take retribution equivalent to the tyranny, the highest way is that of those who overlook and try to resolve the matter through reform. Although choosing reform requires sacrificing emotions, its reward is very great, whose responsibility Allah Almighty has taken. This shows that in general individual matters, Allah Almighty loves the method of reform—whether both parties try to reform themselves, or make others its medium, or others intervene to reconcile.

Retribution is Permissible Only Equivalent to the Tyranny

'Indeed, He does not like wrongdoers'—this is a comprehensive warning: Allah does not like wrongdoers. That is, if a person commits tyranny, he is hated in the sight of Allah, and if a person answers some tyranny with greater tyranny, he is also hated. The desirable way in the sight of Allah is to try to reform, and if not, to take retribution equivalent to the tyranny.

"And whoever defends himself after his being wronged - for those there is no cause [for blame] upon them. The cause is only against those who wrong the people and tyrannize upon the earth without right. Those will have a painful punishment." (41-42)

In 'after his being wronged' (ba'da dhulmihi), the verbal noun (masdar) is prefixed to its object, just as is in verse 3 of Surah Ar-Rum: 'and they, after their being overcome, will overcome.'

Replying to Those Who Consider Retribution Contrary to Piety

This is a reply to the doubt of those who consider it a requirement of piety that a person is continuously beaten at the hands of others and does not take any retribution. If anyone takes retribution, he is considered equally guilty. It states: in such matters, the blame is not upon those who take retribution after being wronged, but upon those who commit tyranny against people and show rebellion in the earth without any right.

'without right'—meaning the Creator and Owner of the earth is God, and no one has any share in its creation or management; but their demand is that no one should raise their head before them. It states: the actual criminals are these people, and for them is a painful punishment. If, in response, the oppressed have to take some action, the responsibility for it lies not on the oppressed, but on the wrongdoers who initiated it. Have a look at what we wrote under verse 29 of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

"And whoever is patient and forgives - indeed, that is of the matters [requiring] resolve." (43)

Summary of the Discussion

This verse holds the position of the summary of the discussion, and from it, several matters are clarified. First, although the right to take retribution equivalent to the tyranny is possessed by every person, in the sight of Allah, the highest way is that of those who observe patience and forgive. Second, only those can forgive who possess the attribute of patience. Those who do not possess it cannot play this game. For this reason, people must establish the attribute of patience within themselves.

Third, this is the character of resolve. Those who establish this character within themselves are among those of resolve.

It should be clear that the guidance to these matters was given to the Muslims to face those conditions which they were facing at the hands of the Quraysh close to migration. Until then, the Muslims had neither become an organized political power, nor was proof fully established against the Quraysh. After migration, when the Muslims became an organized power and proof was established against the Quraysh, they were instructed: cut yourselves off from them entirely and continue fighting with them until they surrender before Islam.

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 44–50

The subsequent verses relate to verse 36 above. The sequence of the discourse had reached the point: this life upon which these opponents are infatuated is temporary; the true eternal wealth is the share of those believers who are playing the game for the successes of the Hereafter instead of this temporary life of the world. After this, some verses described the character of the believers so that the discourse became relevant to the situation and it became clear to the Muslims of that time that this glad tidings is for them, and it also became clear to their opponents that the time of victory is near for those who are oppressed at their hands.

After this, the preceding theme is resumed—stating first to console the Prophet (pbuh) that those who are caught under the law of God cannot be guided by anyone else. Their eyes will open when it is too late. Then, as a final warning, the opponents are reminded: the opportunity still remains; if you wish to regain your senses, do so now. If this time passes, it will never return. At the same time, pointing to the primary cause of their pride, this reality is clarified: whatever anyone receives, they receive through His favor; but man is highly ungrateful that he makes God's blessings a means of rebellion against Him... Recite the verses.

9. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"And he whom Allah leaves astray - for him there is no protector after Him. And you will see the wrongdoers, when they see the punishment, saying, 'Is there for return [to the world] any way?'" (44)

Referring to the Sunnah of Allah to Console the Prophet

This is a reference to the Sunnah of Allah which He has appointed regarding guidance and deviation to console the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: those whom Allah Almighty leaves astray due to their bad deeds are deprived of the capability to receive guidance, and those who are deprived of His guidance, no one else can become their protector. No matter how much you try, those upon whom His curse has fallen will not accept guidance.

'And you will see the wrongdoers...'—'wrongdoers' (Zalimun) refers to those who were left astray. By using this attribute, it is clarified: they deserved to be left astray because they did not benefit from the capabilities He had blessed them with, and did not value His guidance, but blindly followed their desires, because of which they became wrongdoers to their own souls. It states: these unfortunate people are highly proud of their way today, but when they face His punishment, they will say with great regret: 'Is there any way to return to the world so that we may earn some virtues to escape from this punishment?'

"And you will see them being exposed to it, humbled from disgrace, looking with a covert glance. And those who had believed will say, 'Indeed, the losers are the ones who lost themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection.' Unquestionably, the wrongdoers are in a constant punishment." (45)

A Style of the Arabic Language

In 'being exposed to it' (yu'radoona 'alayha), the referent of the pronoun is that very punishment which was mentioned in the preceding verse; since it refers to Hell or the Fire, the pronoun is feminine 'by way of meaning'. In this way, the warning of the punishment is explained. This style is well-known in Arabic.

It states: today these people are mocking this punishment in the pride of their successful life, but on the Day they are brought to be cast into it, they will be looking with a covert glance, with their heads held down in disgrace. When any criminal is led to the place of execution, he has no courage to look at the sword of execution openly, but looks with a covert glance at what is about to happen.

Actual Loss is the Loss of the Hereafter

'And those who had believed will say...'—it states that observing their disgrace and destruction, the believers will proclaim: 'Indeed, the losers are those who lost themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection.' The meaning is: these short-sighted people considered us in loss in the world, whereas that was no loss; the actual loss is this loss of the Day of Resurrection in which these unfortunate people are caught.

'Unquestionably, the wrongdoers are in a constant punishment' describes the cause of why the actual loss is that of the Day of Resurrection. It states: open your ears and hear well! The cause is that these wrongdoers will be caught in an eternal punishment which cannot be averted... the meaning is: in this world, any pain or comfort is temporary, but the punishment of the Hereafter is eternal; for this reason, the actual loss is of those who face the loss of the Hereafter.

"And they will not have any protectors to support them besides Allah. And he whom Allah leaves astray - for him there is no way." (46)

'Besides Allah' (min duni-llah) is in the sense of 'against Allah'. The meaning is: on the Day of Judgment, when they are caught in Allah's punishment, none of those partners and supporters of whom they are proud will be of any use against Allah.

'And he whom Allah leaves astray - for him there is no way'—the same theme which passed in verse 44 above, with a slight change of words, is repeated—stating: due to their bad deeds, Allah has left them to wander in deviation, and those who are left to wander by Allah find no path anywhere, neither in this world nor in the Hereafter.

"Respond to your Lord before a Day comes for which there is no averting from Allah. For you there is no refuge that Day, and for you there is no [power of] denial." (47)

In my view, 'from Allah' (minallahi) relates to 'before a Day comes' (min qabli an ya'tiya Yawmun), and 'for which there is no averting' (la maradda lahu) is the attribute of 'Yawm' (Day).

Invitation by Way of Warning

This is an invitation by way of warning. It states: the opportunity still remains; accept the invitation of your Lord to reform your Hereafter. Otherwise, remember that when that Day of your Lord comes which is inevitable, your accepting or not accepting it at that time will be identical. On that Day, you will have no refuge, nor will you be able to deny anything. Whatever comes before you, you will have to bow down before it.

"But if they turn away - We have not sent you, [O Muhammad], over them as a guardian; upon you is only [the duty of] notification. And indeed, when We let man taste mercy from Us, he rejoices in it; but if evil strikes them for what their hands have put forth, then indeed, man is an ingrate." (48)

This addresses the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: if these people accept this invitation, well and good, and if they do not, do not care about them. We have not appointed you over them as a guardian to force them to accept this invitation. Your responsibility is only to deliver the invitation. Exactly this same theme has passed in verse 6.

Pointing to the Primary Cause of Their Turning Away

'And indeed, when We let man...'—this exposes their cause of turning away, showing that they are narrow-minded and ungrateful people. The condition of such humans is: when We bless them with Our favor, they turn away from Us and boast with pride, and if some adversity touches them for their deeds, they become ungrateful. The meaning is: do not expect of them that they will choose the correct path. Today they enjoy favor, whose pride has become a veil for them, and if We make them taste that punishment which they are demanding, they will despair.

"To Allah belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth; He creates what he wills. He gives to whom He wills female [children], and He gives to whom He wills males. Or He makes them [both] chemistry, males and females, and He makes whom He wills barren. Indeed, He is Knowing and Competent." (49-50)

Narrow-Mindedness is Due to Lacking the Correct Belief

This describes that belief, due to lacking which they fell into this narrow-mindedness and ungratefulness. If they had believed in the reality that the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth is in the control of God alone, and He alone creates what He wills and bestows upon whom He wills, they would not have fallen into this state; rather, they would have remained grateful to their Lord in favor and patient in adversity.

After 'He creates what He wills' (Yakhluqu ma yasha'u), 'He gives to whom He wills female [children]...' is the specific after the general—showing that He alone is the One Who gives daughters to whom He pleases, sons to whom He pleases, both sons and daughters to whom He pleases, and leaves childless whom He pleases. He alone is the Knower and Competent, and whatever He does, He does in accordance with His knowledge and His power; no one else has any share in it. For this reason, the duty of the servants is that in all matters, they rely on God alone, and never become arrogant, nor despaired, nor turn to anyone besides Him.

10. Subsequent Theme: Verses 51–53

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, and if you reflect, you will find that this conclusion relates to the Surah as a whole, and has a highly clear connection with the preceding passage. We have been pointing in various places to this characteristic of the style of the Qur'an—that the theme with which the Surah began is also that on which it ends. Thus, in this Surah, see: it began with 'Thus does He reveal to you, [O Muhammad], and to those before you...'. That is: this Qur'an is the same revelation which Allah Almighty sent down upon other prophets and messengers before. Then, He clarified the identical nature of the objective and the medium, and concluded on the theme with which He began. Thus, in the conclusion, almost the same words are used which appeared in the introduction. He stated: 'And thus We have revealed to you an inspiration of Our command...'. This is a highly clear proof that the entire Surah is a single composite whole.

Its connection with the preceding passage is: the preceding passage concluded with the description of the opponents' cause of turning away. Among their causes was: they said: 'If Muhammad's (pbuh) claim is that Allah reveals His word upon him, why does He not speak to us?' The answer to their objection is given in these verses, and the commentary will clarify that this answer is highly comprehensive, complete, and مسکت... Recite the verses in this light.

11. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"And it is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil or that He sends a messenger [i.e., angel] to reveal, by His permission, what He wills. Indeed, He is High and Wise." (51)

Answering an Objection of the Prophet's Opponents

This is a reply to that objection or demand of the opponents which is quoted in the Qur'an in various places in different styles—asking: why does He not speak to us directly if He speaks to him (the Prophet), as he claims? What special distinction does he possess that He blesses him with His speech, while He does not consider us worthy of attention, whereas we are far superior to him in status and wealth!

In reply to this, He stated: no human holds such a status or station that Allah speaks to him directly. He speaks only through revelation, or from behind a veil, or by sending His messenger (an angel) who reveals by His command whatever He wills to His servant whom He chooses for His speech.

'And it is not for any human being' points to the reality that what prevents direct speech with God is, in reality, man's own weakness and his own lack of capability. The Essence of Allah Almighty is so exalted, so great, and the manifestation of such lights and glories that no human can bear to face Him directly. Regarding a glorious prophet like Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), it is mentioned in the Qur'an that when he wished to see Allah Almighty, he received the reply: 'You cannot see Me. Even the mountains cannot bear My glory, so how can you bear it?'

'except by revelation or from behind a veil or that He sends a messenger to reveal, by His permission, what He wills'—meaning if Allah Almighty speaks to any of His servants, He speaks in three ways.

The Different Forms of God's Speech

* One method is of revelation (Wahy)—meaning casting words into the heart. In the Hadiths, this is described as casting into the heart. That is, Allah Almighty casts His word onto the heart of His Prophet, and the Prophet preserves it. The words suggest that this is not in the form of mere thought or idea, but in the form of words which the Prophet hears, understands, and preserves. Here, it is specifically to be kept in mind: this is the method of God's speech with the prophets; therefore, in our view, the opinion of those is incorrect who say that revelation is cast onto the heart only in the form of thought, and the Prophet gives it the garment of words.

* 'or from behind a veil'—the second method is: He speaks from behind a veil. That is, the Prophet hears the word of Allah and His voice, but does not see Him. An example is Allah's speech with Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). In the Torah and the Qur'an, it is mentioned that Allah spoke with Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) but he did not see Him, and in the Qur'an, it is also clarified that Allah Almighty did not speak in this manner with any other prophet besides Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). This honor was achieved only by Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him).

* 'or that He sends a messenger to reveal, by His permission, what He wills'—the third method is: Allah Almighty sends His messenger (an angel), and that angel, by the command of Allah Almighty, casts onto the heart of the Prophet whatever Allah Almighty wills. For example, in Surah Al-Baqarah:

  'Say, "Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel - it is [none but] he who has brought it [i.e., the Qur'an] down upon your heart, [O Muhammad], by permission of Allah..."' (97).

  This same point is stated in Surah An-Nahl as follows:

  'Say, "The Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord in truth..."' (An-Nahl: 102).

These are the three methods of God's speech. Among them, two methods—the first and second—are characterized by the fact that the word is sent down upon the Prophet directly. That is, there is no medium of Hazrat Jibreel between Allah Almighty and the Prophet, and in the third, Hazrat Jibreel is the medium. Among them, two methods—the first and third—are well-known; the second is among the specific honors of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), and is not mentioned regarding any other prophet. If the principle of chronological order is kept in view, it also emerges that the first method holds priority in terms of status.

Only three methods are mentioned here because the question in response to which this verse was revealed required the mention of these three only; but the Qur'an shows that besides these three methods, there are two other methods through which Allah Almighty informs His close servants of His intentions.

Among them, one method is: the angel appears in human form and delivers the message of Allah. An example of this is present in those events of Hazrat Ibrahim, Hazrat Lut, and Hazrat Maryam which have been described in detail in the Qur'an.

The second method is of dream (Ru'ya)—meaning whatever is desired is commanded or shown in a dream by Allah Almighty. For example, the instruction of sacrifice was given to Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in a dream. Regarding the Battle of Badr, the Prophet (pbuh) was shown various events in a dream. A highly important dream has also passed in Surah Al-Isra'.

This shows that there are five methods of Allah Almighty's speech, and our Prophet (pbuh) was blessed with every method except direct speech, which is among the specific honors of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). It should be kept in mind here that Hazrat Musa was also blessed with this honor only once or twice; otherwise, the Torah is a revelation of the same nature as other scriptures.

'Indeed, He is High and Wise'—here He has referred to two of His attributes. One expresses His greatness, height, and elevation. The second points to His wisdom and its requirements—mercy, justice, and the guidance of creation. Combining these two shows that the Essence of Allah Almighty is so high and elevated that neither does He stand in need of speech with anyone, nor does anyone possess such a status as to speak with Him; but along with this greatness, He is Wise, Just, and Merciful; because of this, He blesses His servants with His speech for the guidance of His creation, and chose those methods for this which were mentioned above. The meaning is: those who demand besides this that God speaks to every one of them directly are unaware of the greatness of God and of their own insignificance! Such foolish people will perish at the hands of their own arrogance.

"And thus We have revealed to you an inspiration of Our command. You did not know what is the Book or [what is] faith, but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will of Our servants. And indeed, [O Muhammad], you guide to a straight path - The path of Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Unquestionably, to Allah do [all] matters return." (52-53)

Describing Revelation as 'Rooh' (Inspiration/Spirit)

This addresses the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: in accordance with this well-known method of revelation, We have revealed to you as well an inspiration (Rooh). 'Rooh' refers to revelation. Describing revelation as 'Rooh' points to the reality: this is the source of true life for you and also for those who accept it. In all heavenly scriptures, this expression is used for the word of Allah, because true life, as Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) stated, is achieved not by bread alone, but by the word that comes from God.

'of Our command' (min amrina) is an adjective qualifying this 'Rooh' to remove any doubt. That is: this 'Rooh' is among His unseen affairs. Its actual reality is known only to Allah Almighty or to the messenger who experiences it. Everyone cannot estimate its reality. For this reason, neither should everyone seek to find out its quality, nor should any wise person deny its existence on the basis that he does not feel any such thing within himself. A person who is not acquainted with the pain of love has no right to deny its existence. This theme has passed in Surah Al-Isra':

'And they ask you concerning the soul. Say, "The soul is of the affairs of my Lord. And you have not been given of knowledge except a little."' (Al-Isra': 85).

Have a look at what we wrote under that verse of Surah Al-Isra'.

An important aspect of this will, God willing, be discussed under verses 4-5 of Surah Ad-Dukhan and in Surah Al-Qadr.

'You did not know what is the Book or [what is] faith, but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will of Our servants'—this describes the benefit of this 'Rooh': it is His bestowing of life that previously you were unaware of the Book and faith, but with the light of this revelation, Allah has illuminated your chest in such a way that you have become a walking tower of light among this creation, and Allah guides with this light whomever He pleases among His servants—whom He pleases, meaning those within whom He finds the capability to benefit from this light. Allah blesses them with it, because His every willing is based on His wisdom and His justice. Here, keep in view also: just as not everyone is worthy of the office of prophethood, but Allah chooses those of His servants who are worthy of it, similarly, only those are the holders of this light who possess its capability. This contains the reply to those who said: 'Why did Allah Almighty choose him (Muhammad [pbuh]) among us for prophethood and His speech, why did His eye not fall upon us?'—and consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) is: 'You cannot bless everyone with this light; only those who prove worthy of it will benefit; those who are not worthy, this light will daze them instead of showing them the path.'

The Relation between the Book and Faith is that of Body and Soul

It should be kept in mind here that the relation between the Book and faith is that of body and soul. The Book is entirely the manifestation of faith. In simple words, the Book is, in reality, the description of the requirements of faith; because of this, there is a connection of body and soul between the two. As far as the Book is concerned, you were unaware of it because you were unlettered, but the negation of being unaware of faith is in terms of its details and requirements. That is, you were unaware of the details and requirements of faith, otherwise, the honorable Prophets (peace be upon them) are blessed with the light of their upright nature even before revelation; because of this, a general faith is present within them which is illuminated by the light of revelation to illuminate a world like the sun. Since the relation between the light of nature and the light of revelation is that of a grain of dust and the sun, in comparison, its negation has been made; but it is a grain of that sun, and only those benefit from the light of prophethood who protect this grain. Those who deprive themselves of it remain deprived of the lights of the Prophet.

The mentor-scholar (ra) interprets faith here as wisdom. In his view, faith is the combination of word, deed, and state, and this is the actual reality of wisdom; therefore, the statement: 'you were unaware of the Book and faith' means: 'you were unaware of the Book and wisdom.' Through the word 'faith', wisdom is explained here, as it were.

'And indeed, [O Muhammad], you guide to a straight path...'—this describes the benefit of this light for the creation—stating that Allah Almighty has blessed you with this light, so you, who were unlettered, are now guiding people to the straight path of Allah, to Whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. If people desire success in the Hereafter, they must value your guidance.

'Unquestionably, to Allah do [all] matters return'—this is a warning at the end: open your ears and hear well that to the God Whom belongs everything, all affairs will return; so be prepared to present yourselves and answer before Him.

By the grace of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. Our final call is that all praise belongs to Allah, Lord of the worlds.

Rahman Abad
12 December, 1975 AD.
